options.content:
# W&B GraphQL Errors — Attribution

Answers **"which part of the GraphQL API is failing for this customer, and how do I get to the cause?"**

Every W&B GraphQL request is owned by a **service** (the schema source file that defines the field — e.g. `launch`, `entities`, `artifacts`). A single erroring service usually points to one dependency or resolver.

**Two kinds of numbers on this page — read them differently:**
- **By service / operation (from traces):** rich attribution, but trace-**sampled** — trust the *shape* (which service dominates), not the absolute count.
- **Per operation (from DogStatsD):** **unsampled** — trust these for true error *volume* and rate.

**To find the actual failing request:** use the **Open Traces in Explore** link (top-right), filter `service.name` to gorilla and `error=true`. Latency/error panels also carry exemplar dots — click one to jump to a trace.

panels.title: GraphQL errors/sec (unsampled)
panels.description: From the DogStatsD counter graphql.operation.failed (unsampled). Verify the metric name on a live stack — DogStatsD names may carry a service prefix; if this panel is empty check label_values(__name__) for graphql_operation_failed. NOTE: currently EMPTY — gorilla runs GORILLA_STATSD_PORT=0 (a core/manifest-side setting, not the operator), which disables DogStatsD emission; populates once that port is set.

panels.title: Rate-limited ops/sec
panels.description: From graphql.operation.rate_limited (unsampled). Rate-limiting is throttling, NOT a fault — see the notecard. NOTE: currently EMPTY — gorilla runs GORILLA_STATSD_PORT=0 (a core/manifest-side setting, not the operator), which disables DogStatsD emission; populates once that port is set.

options.content:
**What it shows** — top-line GraphQL error health. First two tiles are trace-derived (sampled: trust the trend, not the exact count); last two are DogStatsD (unsampled: true rate).

**What bad looks like** — errors/sec climbing off a flat baseline, or *distinct erroring services* jumping from ~1 to many (broad outage vs. one bad dependency).

**Do next** — one service dominating → scroll to **Attribution by service** and open its trace. Rate-limited climbing while errors flat → the customer is being throttled (expected under heavy load), not failing.

**What it shows** — which GraphQL **service** (schema source file) and which **operation** are producing errors, from sampled traces. The `graphql.service` label rides on gorilla's spans (core PR #43519).

**What bad looks like** — one service far above the rest = a single dependency/resolver in trouble; errors spread evenly across many services = something shared (DB, auth, rate-limiter).

**Do next** — note the top service/operation, then **Open Traces in Explore** (top-right), filter to gorilla + `error=true` and that operation name, and read the resolver chain / error message on the actual trace. If this panel is empty, the spanmetrics `graphql.service` dimension may not be flowing yet — confirm traces reach the gateway and the collector was restarted after the dimension was added.

panels.title: GraphQL errors per minute, by service (sampled)

options.content:
**What it shows** — the same service attribution over time, stacked so you can see *when* a service started erroring and whether it correlates with a deploy.

**What bad looks like** — a service's band appearing or ballooning at a specific minute.

**Do next** — line up the onset time with the **Service Image Versions** panel on Field Investigation (a rollout?) and with the customer's report. Then drill to a trace from that window.

panels.title: GraphQL success vs failed per sec (unsampled)
panels.description: Unsampled DogStatsD counters graphql.operation.success / .failed. Trust these for true error volume. Verify metric names on a live stack if empty. NOTE: currently EMPTY — gorilla runs GORILLA_STATSD_PORT=0 (a core/manifest-side setting, not the operator), which disables DogStatsD emission; populates once that port is set.

options.content:
**Why two sources?** Traces are **sampled** — great for attribution (which service/operation), unreliable for absolute counts. DogStatsD counters are **unsampled** — the source of truth for *how many* errors and the real error rate.

**Rule of thumb** — use the panels above (traces) to decide *where* the problem is; use these (DogStatsD) to state *how bad* it is to the customer/engineer.

**If these are empty** — the DogStatsD metric name likely differs from `graphql_operation_*` on your stack (possible service prefix). Check `label_values(__name__)`.

panels.title: Top operations by errors (unsampled)
panels.description: Unsampled. graphql.operation.failed carries operation + code (HTTP status) tags but NOT a service tag — for service attribution use the trace-derived panels above. NOTE: currently EMPTY — gorilla runs GORILLA_STATSD_PORT=0 (a core/manifest-side setting, not the operator), which disables DogStatsD emission; populates once that port is set.

options.content:
**What it shows** — the operations with the most errors, split by HTTP code, from the unsampled counter.

**How to read code** — `4xx` is client/auth (SDK version, permissions, bad query); `5xx` is server-side (open a trace, check the service's dependency and pod logs).

**Note** — no `service` column here: the DogStatsD metric doesn't carry it. Cross-reference the operation name against the **service × operation** table above to get its service.

**Get to the cause in three hops:** (1) identify the top service/operation above → (2) **Open Traces in Explore** (top-right), filter `service.name` to gorilla, `error=true`, and the operation → (3) on the trace, follow **Trace → Logs** to see the log lines for that request. The logs panel below is a starting point scoped to GraphQL errors.

panels.title: Recent GraphQL error logs
panels.description: TODO: verify the LogsQL query against a live VictoriaLogs — the verification stack had NO logs in VictoriaLogs (gorilla's OTEL log export wasn't populated), so this panel is empty until logs flow. gorilla mirrors GraphQL service attribution onto logs (field `service`, via graphql_service_attribution.go); confirm field names (`service`, `status`) and adjust the query to your log schema once logs are present.
